title: ‘Saltburn’: Iconic or Disastrous? Plus: Alexander Payne!
format: Sean and Amanda try to work out what 'Saltburn' actually thinks it's about, revisit 'Promising Young Woman' along the way, draft five 'big swing' movies apiece, and Sean talks to Alexander Payne about 'The Holdovers.'

films.title: Wish
films.note: Amanda took her daughter Alice to a 10 a.m. preview screening at the El Capitan and had a great time, reviews and a soft Thanksgiving box office be damned. Chris Pine voices the villain sorcerer Magnifico.

films.title: Saltburn
films.note: Emerald Fennell's follow-up to 'Promising Young Woman,' shot by Lena Sandgren ('Babylon') and set in 2006. Barry Keoghan plays Oxford scholarship student Oliver Quick, who worms his way into the aristocratic Catton family through Jacob Elordi's Felix. Sean thinks it's profoundly hollow but weirdly watchable; Amanda calls it one of the emptiest movies she's seen and still had fun. Also stars Rosamund Pike, Richard E. Grant, Alison Oliver, and Carey Mulligan.

films.title: The Talented Mr. Ripley
films.note: The clearest touchstone, cited repeatedly: Highsmith's cipher of a sociopath versus Saltburn's Oliver, who gets over-explained instead of staying unreadable.

films.title: Purple Noon
films.note: René Clement's earlier Ripley adaptation, named alongside 'Ripley's Game' as proof the character works best when he stays a cipher.

films.title: Ripley's Game
films.note: The John Malkovich version of Ripley, invoked in the same breath as 'Purple Noon.'

films.title: Promising Young Woman
films.note: Amanda rewatched it for this episode and confirmed she still doesn't buy it: Carey Mulligan's revenge-quest motivations never fully connect for her, though she credits Fennell's eye for period detail (the Away suitcase, the YouTube blowjob-lips tutorial cameo). Sean was more forgiving on release but agrees Saltburn is basically its sequel.

segments.heading: Amanda's Big Swings
segments.note: revealed worst to best on air, ordered best-first here
segments.subject: Big swings

films.title: Titanic
films.note: No. 1: "They built a whole ship, you know?"

films.title: Moulin Rouge!
films.note: No. 2 (tie): staging the climax around a tango version of the Police's "Roxanne."

films.title: La La Land
films.note: No. 2 (tie): Damien Chazelle's Oscar-winning musical; Amanda still isn't fully sure how she feels beyond loving the middle two hours.

films.title: Anna Karenina
films.note: No. 3: Joe Wright staging all thousand pages of Tolstoy as a play; Amanda's favorite of his films, ahead of 'Atonement.'

films.title: Yesterday
films.note: No. 4: Richard Curtis and Danny Boyle's only-one-person-remembers-the-Beatles premise.

films.title: Gigli
films.note: No. 5: Martin Brest's Jennifer Lopez/Ben Affleck romantic-comedy-crime hybrid. "It famously didn't work."

segments.heading: Sean's Big Swings
segments.note: revealed worst to best on air, ordered best-first here
segments.subject: Big swings

films.title: JFK
films.note: No. 1: Oliver Stone's conspiracy epic, and the source of the show's running gag about their friend Bill's JFK theories.

films.title: Magnolia
films.note: No. 2: Paul Thomas Anderson's third film. "You don't have to put your whole diary in the movie, but I think that's a big swing."

films.title: A Cure for Wellness
films.note: No. 3: Gore Verbinski's $75 million spa-horror movie that made $8 million; a deranged early Mia Goth performance.

films.title: Southland Tales
films.note: No. 4: Richard Kelly's maligned 'Donnie Darko' follow-up, which has since built a cult following.

films.title: Vanilla Sky
films.note: No. 5: Cameron Crowe's most Cameron Crowe movie, a remake of 'Open Your Eyes' with Penélope Cruz.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: Written with David Hemingson. Shot at 1.66:1 for richer close-ups; Payne says he approached it as "making a contemporary movie, just pretending I was in 1970," not consciously imitating '70s style. Discusses discovering Dominic Sessa and calls Paul Giamatti a movie star "with no comps."

films.title: Sideways
films.note: His prior Giamatti collaboration; its success made financing 'The Descendants' easier.

films.title: About Schmidt
films.note: Jack Nicholson attached, and still passed on by the studio Payne won't name for being "an expensive, depressing movie"; New Line ended up making it.

films.title: The Descendants
films.note: George Clooney's presence made this one "less hard" to finance.

films.title: Nebraska
films.note: Written with Bob Nelson, cited alongside Hemingson as a writer Payne says he essentially directs through the process.

films.title: Election
films.note: Named in Sean's cold-open intro of Payne alongside 'Sideways.'

films.title: Downsizing
films.note: Payne's prior big swing; he pushes back on the idea 'The Holdovers' was a reaction to it, saying the script was simply next in line.

films.title: Double Indemnity
films.note: The last great thing Payne says he's seen, rewatched with his mother on TCM. "What a movie! What dialogue!"

films.title: It's a Wonderful Life
films.note: Comes up as the Christmas-season classic that hits differently after having kids.
